summary:
Angkor Wat was constructed during the reign of the Khmer King Suryavarman II (r. 1113–c. 1150 AD), built over approximately three decades as both the king's state temple and eventual mausoleum, and represents the largest religious monument ever constructed — covering approximately 400 acres and oriented to the west (unusual for Hindu temples, suggesting funerary purpose) in a design symbolizing Mount Meru, the cosmic mountain at the center of Hindu cosmology. The temple's five towers represent Meru's five peaks; its surrounding moat represents the cosmic ocean; and its concentric galleries, bas-reliefs, and ascending levels create a symbolic journey from the earthly realm to the divine. The construction required an estimated 5–10 million tons of sandstone, quarried from the Kulen Hills some 37 kilometers away and floated down canals, with an estimated workforce of 300,000 workers and 6,000 elephants.

Angkor Wat's bas-reliefs are among the greatest achievements of world art: the gallery walls contain nearly 800 meters of carved narrative reliefs depicting scenes from the Hindu epics (Mahabharata, Ramayana), the churning of the cosmic ocean, Suryavarman II's military campaigns, and the heavens and hells of Hindu cosmology, executed with extraordinary detail and aesthetic sophistication. Unlike most Angkor temples dedicated to Shiva, Angkor Wat was dedicated to Vishnu — reflecting Suryavarman II's personal devotion. The [[event:2249|Khmer Empire's]] capital region of Angkor, of which this temple was the centerpiece, was the largest pre-industrial city in the world at its peak, housing an estimated 750,000–1 million people. Angkor Wat was never abandoned after the Khmer capital shifted in the 15th century — Buddhist monks maintained continuous occupation — and it remains Cambodia's most sacred site and national symbol.
